    --replication-group-id anihpj-cluster-cache \
    --replication-group-description "Cluster mode cache" \
    --engine redis \
    --cache-node-type cache.m7g.large \
    --num-node-groups 3 \  # 3 shards
    --replicas-per-node-group 2 \  # 2 replicas per shard
    --cache-parameter-group-name default.redis7.cluster.on

# Total nodes: 3 shards × (1 primary + 2 replicas) = 9 nodes
# Total capacity: 3 × 6.38GB = ~19GB
# Throughput: 3x single node (parallel operations)
    📊 Monitoring & Insights
    📈 CloudWatch Metrics
    Key Metrics to Monitor:
    Metric Threshold Action
    CPUUtilization > 75% Scale up or add replicas
    DatabaseMemoryUsagePercentage > 80% Scale up or enable eviction
    CacheHitRate < 80% Review caching strategy
    NetworkBytesIn/Out Near node limit Scale up node type
    Create CloudWatch Alarms:
    # High CPU alarm
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
    --alarm-name anihpj-cache-high-cpu \
    --alarm-description "ElastiCache CPU > 75%" \
    --metric-name CPUUtilization \
    --namespace AWS/ElastiCache \
    --statistic Average \
    --period 300 \
    --threshold 75 \
    --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
    --evaluation-periods 2 \
    --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:elasticache-alerts

# Low cache hit rate alarm
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
    --alarm-name anihpj-cache-low-hit-rate \
    --metric-name CacheHitRate \
    --namespace AWS/ElastiCache \
    --statistic Average \
    --period 300 \
    --threshold 80 \
    --comparison-operator LessThanThreshold \
    --evaluation-periods 2
    🔍 Redis Insights
    # Monitor cache performance in Django
from django_redis import get_redis_connection
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def get_cache_stats():
    """Get Redis statistics"""
    redis_conn = get_redis_connection("default")
    info = redis_conn.info()
    
    stats = {
        'version': info.get('redis_version'),
        'uptime_days': info.get('uptime_in_days'),
        'connected_clients': info.get('connected_clients'),
        'used_memory_human': info.get('used_memory_human'),
        'used_memory_peak_human': info.get('used_memory_peak_human'),
        'total_commands_processed': info.get('total_commands_processed'),
        'keyspace_hits': info.get('keyspace_hits', 0),
        'keyspace_misses': info.get('keyspace_misses', 0),
        'evicted_keys': info.get('evicted_keys', 0),
        'expired_keys': info.get('expired_keys', 0),
    }
    
    # Calculate hit rate
    total_requests = stats['keyspace_hits'] + stats['keyspace_misses']
    if total_requests > 0:
        stats['hit_rate'] = round((stats['keyspace_hits'] / total_requests) * 100, 2)
    else:
        stats['hit_rate'] = 0
    
    return stats
    🚨 Alerting & Alarms
    # Create SNS topic for alerts
aws sns create-topic --name elasticache-alerts

# Subscribe email
aws sns subscribe \
    --topic-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:elasticache-alerts \
    --protocol email \
    --notification-endpoint devops@anihpj.com

    💰 5. Pricing & Cost Optimization

    💵 Pricing Models
    Node Type Memory On-Demand (Hourly) Monthly Cost
    cache.t4g.micro 0.5 GB $0.016 ~$11
    cache.t4g.small 1.37 GB $0.034 ~$24
    cache.m7g.large 6.38 GB $0.162 ~$116
    cache.r7g.xlarge 26.32 GB $0.420 ~$301

    Additional Costs:

    • Data transfer: $0.01/GB out to internet
    • Backups: $0.023/GB-month (S3 storage)
    • No charge for data transfer within same AZ
    💡 Cost Strategies
    • Use Reserved Nodes: Save up to 55% vs on-demand
    • Right-size instances: Monitor memory/CPU usage
    • Use T4g instances: Cheaper for variable workloads
    • Enable compression: Reduce memory usage
    • Optimize TTLs: Prevent memory bloat
    • Delete unused clusters: Dev/test environments
    📊 Reserved Nodes
    # Purchase reserved node (1-year commitment)
aws elasticache purchase-reserved-cache-nodes-offering \
    --reserved-cache-nodes-offering-id 1234abcd-12ab-34cd-56ef-1234567890ab \
    --reserved-cache-node-id anihpj-reserved-node-1 \
    --cache-node-count 3

# Savings example:
# On-demand cache.m7g.large: $116/month × 12 = $1,392/year
# Reserved (1-year): ~$880/year
# Savings: ~$512/year (37% discount)
    💰 anihpj Monthly Cost Estimator
    🌱 Starter (Dev/Test)
    Setup:
    • 1× cache.t4g.micro (0.5GB)
    • No replication
    • 10GB data transfer/month
    Monthly Cost: ~$11
    Perfect for testing
    🚀 Production (Recommended)
    Setup:
    • 1× cache.m7g.large (6.38GB) Primary
    • 1× cache.m7g.large Replica
    • Multi-AZ failover
    • 50GB data transfer/month
    Monthly Cost: ~$236
    High availability included
    🏆 High Traffic
    Setup:
    • 3-shard cluster mode
    • 3× cache.r7g.large Primary
    • 3× cache.r7g.large Replicas
    • 200GB data transfer/month
    Monthly Cost: ~$1,018
    Enterprise scale
    💡 Cost Savings Tips: Use Reserved Nodes for 37-55% savings | Right-size based on metrics | Enable compression | Optimize TTLs

    💼 9. Common Use Cases

    🔐 Session Management
    # settings.py - Redis session backend
SESSION_ENGINE = 'django.contrib.sessions.backends.cache'
SESSION_CACHE_ALIAS = 'default'
SESSION_COOKIE_AGE = 7200  # 2 hours

# Store session data
request.session['user_preferences'] = {
    'theme': 'dark',
    'job_alerts': True,
    'location': 'New York'
}
    🔍 Database Query Caching
    def get_trending_jobs():
    """Cache expensive trending jobs query"""
    cache_key = 'trending:jobs:daily'
    
    jobs = cache.get(cache_key)
    if not jobs:
        # Expensive query with aggregations
        jobs = Job.objects.filter(
            is_active=True,
            created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(days=7)
        ).annotate(
            score=F('views') * 2 + F('applications') * 10
        ).order_by('-score')[:20]
        
        cache.set(cache_key, list(jobs), timeout=600)  # 10 min
    
    return jobs
    🏆 Leaderboards & Counters
    from django_redis import get_redis_connection

def update_employer_leaderboard(employer_id, job_count):
    """Update top employers leaderboard using sorted sets"""
    redis_conn = get_redis_connection("default")
    
    # Add employer to leaderboard with score = job_count
    redis_conn.zadd('leaderboard:employers', {employer_id: job_count})
    redis_conn.expire('leaderboard:employers', 3600)

def get_top_employers(limit=10):
    """Get top employers from leaderboard"""
    redis_conn = get_redis_connection("default")
    
    # Get top N employers with scores (descending order)
    return redis_conn.zrevrange(
        'leaderboard:employers',
        0,
        limit - 1,
        withscores=True
    )
    📢 Pub/Sub Messaging
    # Real-time job notifications using Redis Pub/Sub
from django_redis import get_redis_connection

def publish_new_job(job):
    """Publish new job notification"""
    redis_conn = get_redis_connection("default")
    
    message = {
        'job_id': job.id,
        'title': job.title,
        'company': job.employer.name,
        'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat()
    }
    
    redis_conn.publish('channel:new_jobs', json.dumps(message))

def subscribe_to_new_jobs():
    """Subscribe to new job notifications"""
    redis_conn = get_redis_connection("default")
    pubsub = redis_conn.pubsub()
    
    pubsub.subscribe('channel:new_jobs')
    
    for message in pubsub.listen():
        if message['type'] == 'message':
            job_data = json.loads(message['data'])
            print(f"New job: {job_data['title']}")

    ✨ 11. Best Practices

    🎯 Design Patterns
    • Cache-Aside for reads: Most common, resilient pattern
    • Write-Through for critical data: User profiles, settings
    • Write-Behind for analytics: View counters, metrics
    • Use appropriate TTLs: Balance freshness vs performance
    • Implement circuit breakers: Gracefully handle cache failures
    🔑 Key Naming Conventions
    # Good key naming patterns for anihpj
KEY_PATTERNS = {
    'job_detail': 'job:detail:{job_id}',
    'user_profile': 'user:profile:{user_id}',
    'employer_info': 'employer:info:{employer_id}',
    'search_results': 'search:{query}:{filters_hash}:{page}',
    'leaderboard': 'leaderboard:{category}:{timeframe}',
    'trending': 'trending:{type}:{period}',
    'session': 'session:{session_key}',
}

# Benefits:
# • Easy to identify data type
# • Easy to invalidate related keys
# • Consistent across application
# • Supports wildcards for bulk operations
    💾 Memory Management
    # Configure eviction policy (when memory full)
aws elasticache modify-cache-parameter-group \
    --cache-parameter-group-name anihpj-redis7 \
    --parameter-name-values \
        "ParameterName=maxmemory-policy,ParameterValue=allkeys-lru"

# Eviction policies:
# • noeviction: Return errors when memory full (default)
# • allkeys-lru: Evict least recently used keys
# • volatile-lru: Evict LRU keys with TTL set
# • allkeys-lfu: Evict least frequently used keys
# • volatile-ttl: Evict keys with shortest TTL

# Recommendation for anihpj: allkeys-lru (balanced approach)

    🔧 10. Troubleshooting

    🔌 Connection Issues
    Issue Cause Solution
    Connection timeout Security group rules Verify port 6379 allowed from app
    AUTH failed Wrong password Check Parameter Store value
    TLS handshake error SSL/TLS mismatch Use rediss:// (double s) for TLS
    💾 Memory Pressure
    # Check memory usage
redis-cli -h anihpj-cache.amazonaws.com -a "password" --tls INFO memory

# Clear unused keys
redis-cli -h anihpj-cache.amazonaws.com -a "password" --tls FLUSHDB

# Enable eviction policy
aws elasticache modify-cache-parameter-group \
    --cache-parameter-group-name anihpj-redis7 \
    --parameter-name-values "ParameterName=maxmemory-policy,ParameterValue=allkeys-lru"
    🐌 Slow Queries
    • Use SCAN instead of KEYS: KEYS blocks all operations
    • Avoid large collections: Lists/sets with > 1M elements
    • Use pipelining: Batch multiple commands
    • Monitor slow log: SLOWLOG GET 10
    💬 FAQs
    Q: Can I access ElastiCache from outside VPC?

    No. ElastiCache only supports VPC access. Use VPN or Direct Connect for external access.

    Q: How do I migrate from self-hosted Redis?

    Use Redis replication or export/import RDB snapshots.

    Q: Can I upgrade Redis version?

    Yes, using modify-replication-group with --engine-version parameter.

    ⌨️ 11. API & CLI Reference

    � AWS CLI Commands
    # Create Redis cluster
aws elasticache create-replication-group \
    --replication-group-id anihpj-prod \
    --replication-group-description "Production Redis" \
    --engine redis \
    --cache-node-type cache.m7g.large \
    --num-cache-clusters 3 \
    --automatic-failover-enabled \
    --multi-az-enabled

# List clusters
aws elasticache describe-replication-groups

# Modify cluster
aws elasticache modify-replication-group \
    --replication-group-id anihpj-prod \
    --apply-immediately \
    --cache-node-type cache.r7g.xlarge

# Create snapshot
aws elasticache create-snapshot \
    --replication-group-id anihpj-prod \
    --snapshot-name anihpj-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d)

# Delete cluster
aws elasticache delete-replication-group \
    --replication-group-id anihpj-prod \
    --retain-primary-cluster false
    🐍 Python Boto3
    import boto3

# Initialize ElastiCache client
client = boto3.client('elasticache', region_name='us-east-1')

# Create cluster
response = client.create_replication_group(
    ReplicationGroupId='anihpj-prod',
    ReplicationGroupDescription='Production Redis',
    Engine='redis',
    CacheNodeType='cache.m7g.large',
    NumCacheClusters=3,
    AutomaticFailoverEnabled=True,
    MultiAZEnabled=True,
    EngineVersion='7.1'
)

# Get cluster info
response = client.describe_replication_groups(
    ReplicationGroupId='anihpj-prod'
)

endpoint = response['ReplicationGroups'][0]['NodeGroups'][0]['PrimaryEndpoint']
print(f"Primary endpoint: {endpoint['Address']}:{endpoint['Port']}")

    🔗 12. AWS Integration

    📦 RDS Integration
    Cache Database Query Results

    Use ElastiCache to reduce load on RDS PostgreSQL database.

    # Cache expensive RDS queries
from django.core.cache import cache
from django.db.models import Count

def get_job_statistics():
    """Get job statistics with caching"""
    cache_key = "stats:job-counts"
    
    stats = cache.get(cache_key)
    if stats is None:
        # Expensive query on RDS
        stats = {
            'total_jobs': Job.objects.count(),
            'active_jobs': Job.objects.filter(is_active=True).count(),
            'by_category': Job.objects.values('category__name').annotate(
                count=Count('id')
            )
        }
        cache.set(cache_key, stats, timeout=600)  # 10 minutes
    
    return stats
    🗄️ Lambda Integration
    # Lambda function accessing ElastiCache
import redis
import os

# Connect to ElastiCache from Lambda (must be in same VPC)
redis_client = redis.Redis(
    host=os.environ['REDIS_ENDPOINT'],
    port=6379,
    password=os.environ['REDIS_PASSWORD'],
    ssl=True,
    decode_responses=True
)

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """Process job application and update cache"""
    job_id = event['job_id']
    
    # Increment application counter
    redis_client.incr(f'job:applications:{job_id}')
    
    # Invalidate job detail cache
    redis_client.delete(f'anihpj:job:detail:{job_id}')
    
    return {'statusCode': 200}
    � ECS Integration

    ElastiCache works seamlessly with ECS containers. Configure using environment variables:

    // ECS task definition
{
  "containerDefinitions": [{
    "name": "anihpj-web",
    "image": "anihpj:latest",
    "environment": [
      {
        "name": "REDIS_ENDPOINT",
        "value": "anihpj-prod.abc123.use1.cache.amazonaws.com"
      }
    ]
  }]
}
    � CloudWatch Integration

    Monitor ElastiCache metrics and set up alarms in CloudWatch.

    # Create CloudWatch alarm for high memory usage
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
    --alarm-name anihpj-redis-high-memory \
    --alarm-description "Redis memory usage > 80%" \
    --metric-name DatabaseMemoryUsagePercentage \
    --namespace AWS/ElastiCache \
    --statistic Average \
    --period 300 \
    --evaluation-periods 2 \
    --threshold 80 \
    --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
    --dimensions Name=CacheClusterId,Value=anihpj-prod-001

    🚚 13. Migration to ElastiCache

    🏠 Self-Managed to AWS
    # Step 1: Export data from self-managed Redis
redis-cli --rdb /tmp/redis_backup.rdb SAVE

# Step 2: Upload to S3
aws s3 cp /tmp/redis_backup.rdb s3://anihpj-migration/redis_backup.rdb

# Step 3: Create ElastiCache cluster from snapshot
aws elasticache create-snapshot \
    --snapshot-name anihpj-migration-snapshot \
    --replication-group-id anihpj-prod-redis

# Step 4: Update Django to point to new endpoint
REDIS_ENDPOINT = "anihpj-prod.abc123.use1.cache.amazonaws.com"
    Migration Best Practices:
    • Test migration in staging environment first
    • Use dual-write pattern during transition
    • Monitor cache hit rates after migration
    • Plan for minimal downtime (< 5 minutes possible)

    �️ 14. Compliance & Governance

    📜 Compliance Certifications
    AWS ElastiCache Compliance & Certifications:

✅ SOC 1/2/3 - Service Organization Control
✅ PCI DSS Level 1 - Payment Card Industry
✅ ISO 27001/27017/27018 - Information Security
✅ HIPAA - Healthcare Insurance Portability
✅ GDPR - General Data Protection Regulation
✅ FedRAMP - Federal Risk Authorization Program
✅ FISMA - Federal Information Security Management

For anihpj Job Portal:
• GDPR compliance required if serving EU users
• Data encryption at rest and in transit
• Regular security audits
• User data access controls and logging
• Right to erasure (delete user cache on request)
    🔐 Security Summary
    AWS Well-Architected Framework - Security Pillar for ElastiCache:

1. Identity & Access Management
   ✓ Use IAM roles for ElastiCache API access
   ✓ Enable Redis AUTH tokens
   ✓ Use Redis ACLs for fine-grained access
   ✓ Store credentials in AWS Secrets Manager

2. Detective Controls
   ✓ Enable CloudWatch Logs
   ✓ Monitor with CloudWatch metrics
   ✓ Set up SNS alerts for anomalies
   ✓ Regular security audits

3. Infrastructure Protection
   ✓ Deploy in private VPC subnets
   ✓ Use Security Groups for network isolation
   ✓ Enable VPC Flow Logs
   ✓ Use PrivateLink for AWS service access

4. Data Protection
   ✓ Enable encryption at rest (AES-256)
   ✓ Enable encryption in transit (TLS 1.2+)
   ✓ Use AWS KMS for key management
   ✓ Regular automated backups

5. Incident Response
   ✓ Documented incident response plan
   ✓ Automated failover testing
   ✓ Regular disaster recovery drills
   ✓ Post-incident analysis and improvements

    📖 Glossary
    Cache-Aside
    Application checks cache first; on miss, loads from database and populates cache
    Cluster Mode
    Redis configuration with data partitioned across multiple shards for horizontal scaling
    Eviction Policy
    Strategy for removing keys when memory is full (e.g., LRU, LFU)
    Failover
    Automatic promotion of replica to primary when primary node fails
    Hit Rate
    Percentage of cache requests that return data (hits / (hits + misses))
    Multi-AZ
    Deployment across multiple Availability Zones for high availability
    Pipeline
    Batching multiple Redis commands to reduce network round-trips
    Replication Group
    Primary node and one or more read replicas managed as a unit
    Shard
    Partition of data in cluster mode, contains primary and replicas
    TTL (Time-To-Live)
    Duration before a cache key expires and is automatically deleted
    Write-Behind
    Write to cache immediately, persist to database asynchronously
    Write-Through
    Write to cache and database simultaneously

    Comprehensive AWS Services documentation for cloud developers and architects — Featuring real-world examples, best practices, and implementation guides for building scalable and secure cloud applications.
